text: Shunryu Suzuki, who brought Soto Zen to San Francisco in the 1960s, described the meditating mind as a swinging door: breath comes in, breath goes out, and thoughts move through without obstruction. The door does not grab what passes. This image addresses a common confusion about anicca practice: students believe they must empty the mind of distractions, whereas Suzuki taught that trying to close the door — to stop thoughts — converts a flowing process into a frozen one. Impermanence requires movement. When the practitioner stops grasping at stillness as a goal, each arising thought becomes evidence of the very impermanence the teaching points to.
question: What error does Suzuki identify in students who try to empty the mind of distraction?

options.text: They are applying Theravada techniques incompatible with Zen.
Trying to stop thoughts converts a flowing process into a frozen one.
They focus on the breath instead of the arising thought.
They mistake the swinging door for the goal of practice.
